segments.s440-g41: تن کشته is a noun phrase meaning “the slain body” (تن = body, کشته = slain) with the adjective/participle following the noun, typical of Persian poetry. از چاه باز آورد is a verb phrase: از چاه = “out of the well,” and باز + آورد = “brought back” (باز as adverb ‘back/again’ modifying آورد). The subject (he) is omitted and is carried over from the narrative.
segments.s440-g42: جهان را is the definite direct object (را marks ‘the world’). به زاری نیاز آورد is a causative/result clause: به زاری = “to/into lamentation,” نیاز آورد = “caused to need / brought to supplication” (آورد = bring/cause). Together: “he caused the world to fall into lamentation/supplication.”
segments.s440-g43: چون here functions as a temporal conjunction ‘when/as’ (poetic usage). پیش کابل رسید is an Arrival clause: پیش کابل = ‘near/at Kabul’ (پیش used archaically to indicate presence/position toward Kabul) and رسید is simple past ‘arrived/reached.’
segments.s440-g44: به شهر اندرون = ‘into/at the inner part of the city’ (اندرون = inner quarters). نامداری is the noun ‘renown/fame’ and ندید is negative past ‘did not see’ (subject = Farāmarz); thus the clause means he found no renown in the inner city. Note there is no object marker را on نامداری, but it is understood as the object of ندید.
segments.s440-g45: گریزان همه شهر is a verbless/clausal description: گریزان (participle/adjective) ‘fleeing’ modifies همه شهر ‘the whole city/everyone in the city.’ گریان شده is a compound predicate ‘had become weeping’ (گریان = weeping, شده = become/has become), describing the city’s state. Both participial adjectives are used in parallel to paint the scene.
segments.s440-g46: ز سوک جهانگیر = ‘from/because of the mourning for Jahangir’ (ز = poetic ‘from/because of’, سوک = mourning/dirge, جهانگیر here is a proper name). بریان شده is a participial/passive-like phrase ‘was burned/consumed’ used metaphorically to mean being consumed by grief (بریان + شده).
segments.s440-g47: بیامد is simple past ‘came.’ بران (بر + ان) is a demonstrative/adverbial ‘to/onto that’ (referring back to something just mentioned), so بران دشت نخچیرگاه = ‘to that plain, the hunting-ground.’ دشت is ‘plain’ and نخچیرگاه ‘hunting-ground’ apposes it.
segments.s440-g48: به جایی کجا = ‘to a place where’ (جایی … کجا is a poetic relative/locative construction). کنده بودند is the past participle + auxiliary meaning ‘they had dug’ (کنده = dug, بودند = were/had), and چاه is the object ‘a well.’ Word order places the relative word کجا after جایی, a common poetic structure.
segments.s440-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. روی پدر = 'the father's face' (روی = face + پدر = father) is the direct object; دید is the past tense verb 'saw/beheld'. پور دلیر ('the brave son') is the subject placed after the verb, a common poetic word order (verb before subject).
segments.s440-g50: خروشی is the noun 'a roar' (object); برآورد is a past verbal form meaning 'he raised/uttered' (here 'let out'). بر سان شیر is a simile: بر + سان = 'like/as' and شیر = 'lion', so 'like a lion'. The verb governs the noun خروشی and the following simile describes the manner.
segments.s440-g51: بدان گونه is a fixed phrase 'in that manner/thus' (ب + دان + گونه functioning together). بر خاک = 'on/onto the earth' (بر + خاک). تن پر ز خون = 'a body full of blood' where پر ز + noun means 'full of [noun]' (پر = full, ز = 'from/of'). Word order places the descriptive phrase around تن.
segments.s440-g52: به روی زمین = 'upon the ground' (به + روی + زمین). بر فگنده is a participial description 'thrown' or 'cast' (فگنده, past participle) introduced by بر, and نگون ('overthrown/ill‑fated') further qualifies the state. Together they describe the body's condition on the ground.
segments.s440-g53: همی گفت is a poetic expression for 'he said' with همی adding an iterative or emphatic flavor; گفت here is the speech verb (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense). کای is the vocative 'O', addressing پهلوان بلند ('lofty/tall champion') — an epithet where بلند is 'lofty' or 'great'.
segments.s440-g54: به رویت literally 'before your face' = 'in your presence' (به + رویت). که introduces a content clause 'that/who', and آورد is the past tense 'brought/caused'. زین سان = 'of this sort/so' (زین + سان) and گزند = 'harm'. The clause asks/remarks who or what has caused such harm in the addressee's sight.
segments.s440-g55: که here introduces the clause expressing a wish/curse. نفرین = 'curse'. بران ('on/against that') points the curse at the specified man. مرد بی‑باک = 'the fearless man' (مرد = man, بی‑باک = fearless). باد at the end is the optative particle 'may' expressing the wish.
segments.s440-g56: به جای = 'in place of' (به + جای). کله = 'head' (literally skull). بر سرش = 'upon his head' (بر + سر + ش = his). خاک = 'dust' and the final باد is again the optative 'may'. The full phrase is a curse: 'may dust be upon his head instead of a head.'
segments.s440-g113: به باغ اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/into the inner garden' (به + place noun + اندرون as 'inner'). دخمه‌ای is indefinite (ـی) 'a tomb/sepulchre'. ساختند is the 3rd-person plural past tense 'they built/they made' and comes at the end as the main verb (SOV order).
segments.s440-g114: سرش را is the definite object ('its head') with the enclitic pronoun ش = 'his/its' and را marking the object. به ابر اندر is a directional/location phrase 'towards/into the cloud(s)'. افراختند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they raised/erected', so the clause says they raised its head up into the clouds.
segments.s440-g115: برابر here functions adverbially as 'opposite / facing' (they set something facing something else). نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they placed / they set' (verb at the end). زرین دو تخت is the noun phrase 'two golden thrones/beds' serving as the object of نهادند.
segments.s440-g116: بران means 'on/upon them' or 'thereon' (referring back to the thrones). خوابنیده is a participial/adjectival form 'having slept / asleep' used as a noun phrase 'the sleeper'. گو here functions in the classical/poetic sense of 'called / so-called' or a linking particle before a name/epithet; نیکبخت is an epithet 'fortunate'. Grammatically this places the sleeping person, named/epithetized 'fortunate', upon the thrones.
segments.s440-g117: هرانکس 'whoever' begins a free relative; که is the relative particle 'who/that'; بود is the past-tense copula 'was'. از + noun here means 'from/among' (i.e. 'among the worshippers'). Word order places the relative first, then its predicate and the phrase marking group membership.
segments.s440-g118: This is a further specification introduced with another از 'among/of' (marking membership). آزاد here means 'free / noble' (free-born or noble in classical Persian). وز is an archaic connective equivalent to و ('and') linking آزاد and پاکدل; since the provided senses don't include 'and', it is marked below as not matching. پاکدل is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted'; بندگان is 'servants / humble ones'. Together: 'among the free and the pure‑hearted servants.'
segments.s440-g119: همی is a classical/epic adverb placed before the verb to give a continuous or habitual flavor ('they did so'), here intensifying the action. مشک is the object 'musk'. باگل is a compound prepositional expression 'with flowers' (با + گل). برآمیختند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they mingled / they mixed together' (verb at the end).
segments.s440-g120: به پای is a locative/directional phrase 'at/to the foot/base (of)'. گو here again functions as the classical 'called / so‑named' (introducing a place-name); پیلتن is a proper name (place). ریختند is the 3rd-person plural past of ریختن 'they poured'. The whole segment says they poured (it) at the foot of the place called Piltan.
segments.s440-g73: نخستین is an adverb meaning 'first' placed before the verb for emphasis. بشستندش is past tense, 3rd-person plural (ب + شستند) with the enclitic object -ش 'him' attached: 'they washed him.' از خون گرم is a prepositional phrase supplying the substance/instrument of washing ('with/from warm blood'); از here functions like an instrumental/partitive preposition.
segments.s440-g74: This is a coordinated list of the places the washing was applied. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' governing the list; the repeated و simply joins the nouns. تنش combines تن 'body' with the enclitic possession ش 'his'. نرم‌نرم is a reduplicated adverb meaning 'softly/gently' modifying the whole action.
segments.s440-g75: همی is a classical/poetic adverb often indicating continuous or habitual action (here rendered 'they then/they proceeded to'). عنبر و زعفران are the direct objects (incense materials). سوختند is the past 3rd-person plural of سوزیدن 'to burn/consume' (here active: 'they burned/caused to burn').
segments.s440-g76: همه marks totality ('all'). خستگیهاش is a plural noun 'his fatigues/weariness' (خستگی + plural -ها + enclitic ش 'his'). بردوختند is the past 3rd-person plural of دوختن with the prefix بر/بـ (here 'they sewed/stitched up'); in context it describes mending/closing (wounds or fatigue-metaphorically), the verb agrees with an implicit agent 'they'.
segments.s440-g77: همی again signals the continuing/iterative action. ریخت is from ریختن used here in the sense of 'pour'/'sprinkle' (an action of pouring/sprinkling). بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. تارکش is a noun in the text; its precise ritual sense here is specialized/poetic and not covered by the minor sense choices given, so the semantic index is -1. بر گلاب places گلاب 'rosewater' after the preposition (poetic word order: 'they poured rosewater upon X').
segments.s440-g78: بگسترد is a past/actionive form meaning 'they spread/laid out' (b- prefix + گستر). بر تنش is the prepositional phrase 'on his body' (تن + enclitic ش 'his'). کافور ناب is a noun phrase 'pure camphor' with ناب 'pure/excellent' as the adjective following the noun (typical Persian order: noun + adjective).
segments.s440-g79: به + noun marks the material/garment here: به دیبا = 'in/with brocade'. تنش is the possessed noun (تن + enclitic -ش = 'his body'), and را is the definite/object marker placed after the direct object before the verb (typical Persian SOV order). بیاراستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they adorned/arrayed' (classical/poetic form related to آراستن; the -ند ending shows plural past narrative).
segments.s440-g80: ازان پس is an adverbial temporal phrase (archaic form of از آن پس) meaning 'after that/thereafter'. گل و مشک و is a rhythmic, coordinate list ('rose and musk and') with the final و kept for poetic effect. می‌خواستند is past plural meaning 'they wanted/were intending' (a past habitual/intentional sense; in context it indicates they proceeded to employ or apply those substances).
segments.s440-g33: کجات is an interrogative adverb 'where' (elliptical question). آن is a demonstrative 'that'. دل و رای و روشن‌روان are three coordinated nouns joined by و (and). The phrase is elliptical — no finite verb — a typical poetic (rhetorical) question: 'Where is that X, Y, and Z?'.
segments.s440-g34: Again کجات introduces a parallel interrogative. آن marks the same demonstrative 'that'. بر و برز و یال are a coordinated trio (nouns/abstracts meaning force/majesty/crest) linked by و, modified by the adjective گران 'heavy/weighty' after them (postpositive modifier). The line keeps an omitted verb understood from the question.
segments.s440-g35: کجات 'where' + آن 'that' again; بزرگ is an attributive adjective 'great'. اژدهافش is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'dragon‑bearing/dragon‑emblazoned' modifying درفش 'banner/standard'. Word order is adjective(s) before the noun, typical Persian poetic order.
segments.s440-g36: کجا is another interrogative 'where'. A simple coordination of nouns (تیر 'arrow', گوپال, تیغ 'sword') linked by و. بنفش postmodifies تیغ ('purple sword'). گوپال here is treated as a named item (proper name) listed among weapons; the line omits a verb, continuing the rhetorical questioning.
segments.s440-g37: نماندی is past tense, 2nd person singular with negative prefix ن-: 'you did not remain'. به گیتی uses به as a locative 'in/within' + گیتی 'the world'. و connects the two clauses; رفتی is the simple past 'you went' and به خاک uses به + خاک 'into the earth/dust'. The two clauses are paratactic (joined by و).
segments.s440-g38: که introduces a purposive/wish clause here; بادا is an optative particle 'may it be' (a formulaic wish). سر دشمنت 'the head of your enemy' is the clause's subject and direct object of the wished verb (implicit 'be placed'). در مغاک 'in the abyss' uses در + noun to give location. The whole clause is a short prayer/wish following the factual clause.
segments.s440-g39: پس انگه is a temporal sequence 'then/thereupon' (پس + آنگه). فرامرز را uses را as the definite direct‑object marker (Faramarz is the one sent). با سپاه is an instrumental/accompanying phrase 'with an army'. The order is typical: temporal phrase — object (marked) — accompaniment.
segments.s440-g40: فرستاد is the past tense, 3rd person singular 'he sent/dispatched'. تا introduces purpose 'so that/in order that' and governs جوید (here a subjunctive/intentional form 'may seek' or 'might seek'), so the clause means 'sent ... so that he might seek battle'. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' (i.e. 'from the king').
segments.s440-g1: ازان (azān) is a poetic compound meaning 'of those / from those' referring back to previously mentioned famous men; it stands before the noun. نامداران (nām-dārān) is the plural noun 'renowned ones' (named + plural). سواری (savārī) is an indefinite noun 'a rider/horseman' as the subject. بجست (be-jast) is the past tense of the verb 'to leap/spring forth' (here with the prefix be-), so the whole clause reads literally 'from those renowned ones a rider leapt forth.'
segments.s440-g2: گهی ... گهی (gahi ... gahi) is a repeated adverb meaning 'now and then / sometimes ... sometimes ...' marking alternation. شد (shod) is the past of شدن 'to become', used here with پیاده (piyāde) 'on foot / dismounted' to mean 'he became on foot' (i.e. dismounted). برنشست (bar-neshast) is a compound past verb 'sat upon / mounted (again)' (bar- + neshast), so the second half means 'sometimes he mounted.' The clause as a whole describes alternating actions.
segments.s440-g3: چو آمد (cho āmad) is a temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = 'when'; آمد = past of 'come'). سوی زابلستان (suy-e Zābulistān) places the motion 'toward Zabulistan' (سوی = 'toward'; زابلستان = the region). بگفت (be-goft) is a past reporting verb 'he said'. Grammatically this is a typical time+motion clause followed by the reported content.
segments.s440-g4: که introduces reported content ('that'). پیل (pīl) is the noun 'elephant'. ژیان (zhiyān) here is used poetically as 'life / living being' or as an epithet attached to پیل to indicate the living elephant; together they refer to the elephant as a living creature. گشت (gasht) is the past of گشتن 'became / turned' (here 'became' or 'came to be'). با خاک جفت is an idiomatic poetic phrase 'paired with the earth' (با = 'with', خاک = 'earth/soil', جفت = 'pair') meaning 'laid with the earth' — i.e. fallen to the ground or dead. The whole clause reports that the elephant lay with the earth.
segments.s440-g5: زواره (Zowāra) here is used as a proper name (personal name) and is the subject; همان (hamān) after it means 'the same / likewise / there also' (i.e. 'Zowara himself'). و سپاهش همان links with و 'and' plus سپاهش (sepāh‑aš = 'his army', سپاه + possessive suffix ش) and همان again 'the same/also' — together: 'Zowara and his army likewise (were/stood).'
segments.s440-g6: سواری (savārī) 'a rider/horseman' is the subject. نجست (najas-t) here is the negative past form 'did not spring/leap forth' (the verb جستن 'to spring' with the negative prefix ن-). از بد بدگمان is an ablative/cause phrase 'because of bad mistrust' (از ... = 'out of / because of', بد = 'bad', بدگمان = 'suspicious / ill-suspecting'). So the clause says no horseman came forward owing to ill suspicion.
segments.s440-g7: خروشی (kharūshī) is a noun 'a shout/roar/commotion'. برآمد (bar-āmad) is 'arose/issued forth'. ز زابلستان (ze Zābulistān) is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from Zabulistan' (ز = 'from', زابلستان = the region). The clause states the origin and nature of the noise.
segments.s440-g8: ز (ze) again 'from'. بدخواه (bad-khah) is 'ill-wisher / enemy' (one who wishes ill). وز شاه کابلستان is parsed as two prepositional elements joined (و + ز) meaning 'and from', introducing the next source; however the lemma list supplied for وز does not match this connective use, so the appropriate sense cannot be selected from the supplied options. شاه کابلستان (shāh-e Kābulistān) is 'the king of Kabulistan'. The whole segment lists who the clamor came from.
segments.s440-g97: برفتند is a past plural verb (‘they went/departed’) placed at the start in an older poetic word order. بیداردل is a post-verbal epithet/appositive describing the people who left ('the wakeful‑hearted' or grieving ones). درگران functions adverbially meaning 'elsewhere' or 'to other places', completing the clause.
segments.s440-g98: بریدند is a past transitive verb ('they cut/they tore off'). ازو is the archaic fused prepositional form 'from him/it' (from + third person). تختهای گران is the direct object: تختهای ('thrones/couches') qualified by گران — here 'precious/heavy' — so the whole phrase means 'his heavy/precious thrones' which were cut away or taken from him.
segments.s440-g99: This clause has an older/topical word order: دو روز ('two days') gives the temporal frame, اندر‌ان ('in/therein') links to the previous matter, and کار ('affair/work') names the concern; شد is the past copula/impersonal 'it became/was', so the clause means 'for two days the time was occupied with that affair.'
segments.s440-g100: تن رخش is the direct object (the body of Rakhsh — رخش being the horse's name), placed before the verb in the poetic order. بر + پیل کردند بار is a periphrastic construction: بر پیل کردند 'they put/placed onto an elephant' with بار ('a load') functioning as the resulting status — 'they made (it) a load on an elephant', i.e. 'they loaded Rakhsh's body onto an elephant.'
segments.s440-g101: ز is the poetic variant of از ('from'), and the phrase ز کابلستان تا به زابلستان marks the extent/range 'from Kabulistan as far as Zabulistan'; تا به here works as 'up to / as far as.'
segments.s440-g102: زمین شد is a simple past copular clause ('the land became'). به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner of / like,' and غلغلستان ('a place of tumult/noise') is the simile complement: 'the land became like a place of tumult.'
segments.s440-g103: زن و مرد is the simple noun pair 'women and men.' بد ایستاده is a participial/adverbial phrase where بد ('ill/afflicted') qualifies ایستاده ('standing') — together 'standing sorrowfully/ill at ease.' به پای means 'at the feet (of someone),' so the clause describes people standing sorrowfully at his feet. The word order (noun + participle) follows classical Persian style.
segments.s440-g104: تنی را 'a body' (indefinite here) is the object marked by را for emphasis; نبد is the archaic negative existential ('there was not'). بر زمین نیز جای literally 'even on the ground a place' — with نیز ('even/also') adding emphasis — so the clause means 'there was not even room on the ground for a body.' Note the inverted/emphatic word order and the archaic negative form نبد.
segments.s440-g89: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (marks origin/place). جاهی here means 'place/seat' (not 'rank'); برادرش را is the definite object ('his brother' + object marker را). برکشید is a past tense verb (3rd person) 'raised/lifted'. The clause order is prepositional phrase + object + verb.
segments.s440-g90: همی is a narrative particle placed before the verb to indicate immediacy/continued action. دوخت is a past-tense verb (3rd person); in epic usage it can mean 'moved/ran/hurried' or 'fastened' depending on context (here narrative sense 'hurried to'). جایی کجا forms a fused locative/relative clause 'to the place where...' and خسته دید is a simple past clause 'he saw (him) weary' with the object implicit.
segments.s440-g91: زبر here means 'above/on top' (not 'coarse'); it governs the following nouns as what lies on top. The sequence مشک و کافور lists materials 'musk and camphor' and و is simple coordination. زیرش is زیر + enclitic ش 'under it/beneath it', and گلاب names 'rosewater'.
segments.s440-g92: ازان سان is a fixed adverbial 'in that way/thus.' همی again is the narrative particle before the verb. ریخت is the past verb 'poured' (here the action of sprinkling/pouring liquids). بر جای خواب is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the place of sleep' (i.e. on the bed).
segments.s440-g93: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereupon.' تن رخش is a noun phrase 'the body of Rakhsh' (تن = 'body', رخش = the steed's name). را marks this phrase as the definite object, and برکشید is the past tense 'he lifted/raised.'
segments.s440-g94: بشست is a past-tense verb 'he washed.' و coordinates actions. برو here functions as an adverbial/transition meaning 'thereupon/then' in narrative (though its basic lemma can mean 'go'). جامه‌ها گسترید is the verb phrase 'he spread the garments' (جامه‌ها = plural 'garments', گسترید = past 'spread').
segments.s440-g95: بشستند is a 3rd-person plural past 'they washed.' و coordinates clauses. کردند is 'they made/did' and takes the following noun phrase دیبا کفن as its object: 'a shroud of brocade' (دیبا = brocade, کفن = shroud). The word order is verb (they made) + object.
segments.s440-g96: بجستند here is past-tense 'they sought' (not 'sprang'); جایی is 'a place/somewhere.' یکی functions as an indefinite determiner 'a certain/one' modifying نارون (an elm tree). The clause reads 'they sought a place, a certain elm.'
segments.s440-g25: This is a rhetorical question. چرا is the interrogative 'why', followed by بایدم (old poetic form of باید + ـم) giving the modal 'must/should I'. زندگانی is the noun 'life', and the coordinating و گاه ('and — for a time') attaches a temporal nuance; the verb is implied by بایدم and applies to both زندگانی and the following phrase.
segments.s440-g26: Parallel structure to the previous segment: چرا + بایدم ('why should I') governs the nominal phrase خواب و آرامگاه. خواب here means 'sleep' (rest/sleep), و links to آرامگاه 'tomb/resting-place', so the whole asks why one should sleep (i.e. die) and be put in a grave.
segments.s440-g27: پس‌انگه 'then/afterwards' sets the temporal frame. بسی here intensifies مویه, so بسی مویه is 'much/long lamentation'. آغاز کرد is the compound 'began/made a beginning' (آغاز noun + کرد light verb) in the simple past.
segments.s440-g28: چو introduces a subordinate temporal clause ('when'). بر governs پور پهلو (here a proper name or title 'Pour‑Pahlu' — پور functioning as name element), meaning roughly 'upon/at the side of Pour‑Pahlu'. همی is the older continuous/emphatic particle preserved in epic Persian; ساز is 'tune/instrument' and کرد is the light verb 'did/made' — together 'he played/made a tune'.
segments.s440-g29: A sequence of names or epithets presented in apposition; word order is noun/adjectival names placed next to each other as a proper-name phrase. Each element functions as a title or name rather than as a governed syntactic object.
segments.s440-g30: A string of adjectival epithets describing the named figure: دلاور 'valiant/heroic', جهاندیده 'world‑wise/experienced', گندآورا a compound meaning 'bringer of corruption/pestilence' — all stacked without an explicit copula, a common poetic device.
segments.s440-g31: کجات is the interrogative 'where?/from where?', followed by the demonstrative آن 'that' pointing to the qualities. دلیری و مردانگی are coordinated abstract nouns 'bravery and manliness/valor'. The structure asks for the whereabouts/existence of those qualities.
segments.s440-g32: Repeats the same interrogative structure (کجات آن ...) now applied to بزرگی 'greatness' and فرزانگی 'wisdom/sagacity'. The pairing emphasizes loss or absence of noble qualities.
segments.s440-g57: This is an oath-formula introduced by the preposition به meaning "by" (used adverbially here). یزدان is the object of به (= “by God”), then جان تو is another oath-phrase “by your life” with تو in vocative (تو‌ای = “you, O…”), and نامدار is a vocative adjective “renowned” modifying the addressee. Word order places the divine oath first, then the personal (vocative) address.
segments.s440-g58: Another parallel oath phrase introduced by به “by”; خاک here means “dust” (an oath invoking humiliation or burial), followed by the proper names نریمان and سام, with سوار (“rider/horseman”) qualifying سام. The sequence names the objects/invocations of the oath.
segments.s440-g59: که introduces a subordinate clause “that”; هرگز is the negative adverb “never”; نبیند is a third-person negative-form verb meaning “(shall) see” (here used in the sense of ‘will not be seen’); تنم is “my body” (تن + enclitic -م) and is the patient of نبیند; جز زره expresses the exception: “except armor” (i.e. only seen in armor). Note the verb precedes the object (verb-subject/object order typical in classical/poetic Persian).
segments.s440-g60: This is a terse adjectival phrase modifying some element of the preceding clause (most naturally the body or its trappings). بیوسنده is an archaic/rare adjective (here best taken as “unbound/unsupported” or otherwise uncertain in exact sense) coordinated with برفگنده “snow-laden,” and both qualify گره “knot.” The conjunction و simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s440-g61: بدان is the imperative “know!” (ب as prefix + دان “know/consider”); تا که introduces the content clause “that/so that”; کین is the noun “vengeance/enmity”; گو here follows کین and functions adjectivally/participially (kīn-gu = “bearing/bringing vengeance” or “of vengeance”), and پیلتن is a place-name or destination. Together they form a phrase meaning roughly “toward Piltan bearing vengeance” or “to Piltan for vengeance.”
segments.s440-g62: بخواهم is first-person finite (subjunctive/future-ish) of خواستن, here “I will seek / I shall demand.” ازان is a compound prepositional demonstrative “from that / from it” and introduces the source/target of the action; بی‌وفا is the adjective “faithless / disloyal” modifying انجمن “assembly/meeting.” Word order places the verb first and the prepositional object after it (typical in older Persian prose/poetry).
segments.s440-g63: هم‌انکس is an emphatic demonstrative/relative meaning “that very person”; که is the relative pronoun “who”; با او “with him” marks accompaniment; بدین is a classical composition meaning “in/with this” (here introducing the phrase بدین کین میان = “in this enmity between [them]”); کین is “enmity/vengeance” and میان means “between.” Altogether: “that very one who, with him, in this enmity between them…”
segments.s440-g64: ببستند is a third-person plural past tense meaning “they bound/they concluded (a pact)” — here it means they made/tied the enmity; و آمد is “and came” (third-person past); به ما is the prepositional phrase “to us”; بر زبان literally “on the tongue” = “in speech” or “uttering/with the tongue,” so آمد به ما بر زبان = “came to us speaking of it / reported it to us.”
segments.s440-g105: Word order is object + locative phrase + verb: 'دو تابوت' (two coffins) is the direct object, 'بر دست' is a prepositional phrase literally ‘on (their) hand(s)’ i.e. 'in their hands', and 'بگذاشتند' is the past tense 3rd-person plural (archaic prefixed form of گذاشتند) meaning 'they placed/took up'.
segments.s440-g106: 'ز انبوه' ('from/out of the crowd' — z = poetic 'from/ out of'), 'چون' = 'like', and 'باد' = 'wind'. 'پنداشتند' is past 3rd-person plural 'they supposed/imagined'. The clause means that, because of the crowd, they supposed (implicitly him/the body) to have vanished 'like the wind'.
segments.s440-g107: 'بده روز و ده شب' here is a poetic contraction of 'به ده روز و ده شب' — a temporal phrase meaning 'after/for ten days and ten nights'. 'به زابل رسید' = 'reached Zabul' with رسید (past) as the verb of arrival.
segments.s440-g108: 'کسش' here functions negatively as 'no one' (an indefinite negative use). 'بر زمین' = 'onto the ground', 'بر نهاده' is the past participle/compound meaning 'placed/laid upon', and 'ندید' is the past negative 'did not see' (ن- prefix marks negation), so the clause means 'no one saw him laid on the ground'.
segments.s440-g109: 'زمانه' (the times/the world) is subject and 'شد' is the past of شدن 'became' — a change-of-state copula. 'از درد او' = 'because of/from his pain' and 'با خروش' = 'with a cry/uproar'; together they state that the world was changed/struck by his suffering, accompanied by tumult.
segments.s440-g110: 'تو گفتی' is literally 'you said' (تو = 'you', گفتی = 2nd-person past of گفتن) and here functions as a reporting formula introducing indirect speech. 'که' = 'that', 'هامون' is the plain/hamun as subject, 'برآمد' = 'rose/emerged', and 'به جوش' is a locative/descr. phrase 'into a boil/surge' describing how the plain rose.
segments.s440-g111: 'کسی نیز نشنید' = 'no one (also) heard' — 'کسی' here is negative 'no one' (with نیز 'also/either' strengthening the negative), 'نشنید' is past negative of شنیدن. 'آواز کس' = 'the voice of anyone/any voice' (کس = anyone) — object of 'hear'.
segments.s440-g112: 'همه بومها' = 'all the homelands/lands' (plural), 'مویه کردند' = past plural 'made lament' (مویه = lament, کردند = did/made), and 'و بس' is an idiomatic closing 'and that was all' / 'and enough'. Word order is typical: subject + verb + final parenthetical.
segments.s440-g81: کفن‌دوز is the agent noun 'shroud-maker' placed at the front for emphasis. بر وی is a prepositional phrase 'upon him' (بر + وی). ببارید is 3rd-person singular past of باریدن 'to rain/ pour down' and takes خون ('blood') as its notional subject; the verb is used here intransitively with a theatrical word order (agent before the verb phrase).
segments.s440-g82: به شانه زد is a verb phrase 'struck/hit upon the shoulder' where به + noun marks the target ('onto his shoulder'). زد is the past tense 'struck/fell on'. آن ریش کافورگون is the subject placed after the verb (typical in Persian poetry): 'that camphor-colored beard.'
segments.s440-g83: نبد is an archaic negative-existential 'there was not / did not exist.' جا names the missing thing ('a place'). تنش را is 'his body' with the possessive -ش and the object marker را (here marking the definite phrase). همی works as an intensifier/adiitional particle ('even/indeed'), and بر دو تخت 'on two couches/beds' gives the location being negated.
segments.s440-g84: تنی is an adjective/nominal 'bodily / a body' used rhetorically. بود‌گر is a fused conditional/optative 'if it were' (classical form). سایه‌گستر درخت is the complement 'a shade-spreading tree.' The whole line is a rhetorical question contrasting a physical body with a tree (the conditional form makes the comparison hypothetical).
segments.s440-g85: یکی نغز is an indefinite nominal phrase 'a certain fine/delicate (one)'; تابوت کردند is a verb phrase 'they made a coffin' (کردند = made/do). Word order is poetic: the object (تابوت) appears before the verb. ساج at the end specifies the material (saj wood) of the coffin.
segments.s440-g86: This phrase lists fittings/materials. میخ زرین = 'golden nails' (میخ 'nail', زرین 'golden'). The initial برو here is a poetic/elliptical linking element (not the literal 'go'); its precise dictionary sense is not in the provided list, so it's marked as unmatched. و connects to پیکر ز عاج, where پیکر 'panel/figure/lining' + ز (poetic 'from') عاج 'ivory' = 'with an ivory (lining/figure)'.
segments.s440-g87: همه 'all' modifies درزهایش 'its seams' (درز + plural + possessive). گرفته is a past participle/predicate here meaning 'filled/sealed' (classically one might expect a form of بود, which is omitted in poetry). به قیر 'with tar/pitch' gives the material used to seal the seams.
segments.s440-g88: برآلوده is a compound adjective 'smeared/soiled upon' (literally 'stained over'). بر قیر مشک و عبیر lists the substances: 'with tar, musk, and (amber)gris' — بر here introduces what it is smeared with; عبیر in this material list is the substance 'ambergris' rather than the abstract 'fragrance.' The phrase is a catalog of anointments applied to the coffin.
segments.s440-g121: همی is a poetic/perfective-aspect marker here meaning 'kept on' or 'was continually'; هرکسی is the subject 'everyone' (indefinite distributive). گفت is the past 3rd-person form of گفتن 'said' (so the clause means 'everyone said/kept saying'). کای is the vocative 'O', followed by the epithet نامدار 'renowned', addressing the hero directly.
segments.s440-g122: This is a direct question: چرا 'why' introduces the interrogative; خواستی is the past/preterite 2nd-person form of خواستن 'you wanted/did you wish' (embedded as the verb of the question). مشک و عنبر are the two items listed with و 'and' between them; نثار is a verbal noun/infinitival use meaning 'to offer/as an offering' (here part of the object/complement 'to offer musk and amber').
segments.s440-g123: نخواهی is the negative present/future/subjunctive form (n‑ + خواهی) addressing 'you' — here predicting or stating you will not have something. همی again gives a continuous/poetic flavour. پادشاهی is the noun 'kingship/kingdom' (object/predicate), و 'and' links the next nominal بزم 'feast/banquet' as another thing you will not possess/enjoy.
segments.s440-g124: نپوشی is the negative of پوشیدن in the same 2nd-person form as above (you will not put on/wear); همی is the habitual/poetic marker; نیز 'also/likewise' links to the following phrase; خفتان here is an infinitival/verb form from خفتن 'to lie/sleep' (used idiomatically for lying down or falling), and رزم 'battle' is its locative/oblique complement ('in/at battle'). The segment thus contrasts the lack of peaceful feasting with fate on the battlefield.
segments.s440-g125: نبخشی is the negative form of بخشیدن 'to give/bestow' in the 2nd-person (you will not give); همی again is the continuous/poetic marker; گنج 'treasure' and دینار 'dinar(s)' are the coordinated objects (و 'and' links them); نیز 'also' reinforces the listing/contrast.
segments.s440-g126: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily' or 'indeed'. که introduces the result/clause 'that' (here causal/explanatory). شد is the past of شدن 'became' (so 'became'); پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'before you/in your presence' (پیش + تو); خوار چیز literally 'a despised/lowly thing' (خوار 'despised', چیز 'thing') -- the clause says these things became contemptible before you.
segments.s440-g127: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. شاد باشی is an optative/subjunctive blessing: شاد 'happy' + باشی (subjunctive/imperative-form of بودن) = 'may you be happy'. به before خرم بهشت here marks the destination/state 'in/to the joyful Paradise' (به + adjective + noun), with خرم 'joyful' qualifying بهشت 'paradise'.
segments.s440-g128: که introduces the explanatory/casual clause 'for/because'. یزدانت is یزدان + -ت 'your God/divinity' (possessive). از is the preposition 'from/of'. داد here is the noun 'justice' (not the verb). و links the two qualities; مردی is an abstract noun 'manliness/courage'. سرشت is the past 3rd-person form of سرشتن 'to create/form' (i.e., 'he formed/created'), so the clause means 'for your God created you of justice and manliness'.
segments.s440-g129: در دخمه is a prepositional phrase ('in the tomb/vault') placed at the clause start. بستند and گشتند are past‑tense, 3rd person plural verbs ('they closed' / 'they went/turned away'); the subject is omitted as is normal in Persian. باز follows گشتند as an adverb meaning 'back/away/again' (classical order: verb + adverb).
segments.s440-g130: شد is a past/resultive verb here ('became/appeared'); آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'; the adjective نامور follows آن and precedes the noun شیر ('renowned lion'); گردن‌فراز is a postpositive epithet ('proud, high‑necked') modifying شیر. The clause has the normal classical order of predicate + demonstrative + adjectival phrase.
segments.s440-g131: This begins with the interrogative چه ('what') followed by جویی (classical 2nd‑person present 'you seek' / 'do you seek'); همی is an archaic continuous/present marker attached to the verb. زین = ز + این ('from this') is a contracted prepositional phrase, and سرای سپنج is a noun phrase 'house/dwelling of sponge' with the qualifier سپنج after سرای (poetic order).
segments.s440-g132: کز is the classical compound preposition/conjunction ('from which' / 'that from'); آغاز رنجست is a nominal clause where رنجست = رنج + است ('is suffering/pain'); و joins the coordinated second member, where the copula is omitted before فرجام (elliptical construction): 'whose beginning is suffering and (whose) end (is) suffering.'
segments.s440-g133: بریزی here has an optative/conditional force ('you would cast/throw'); به خاک is the goal 'to the earth/dust'. ار is the archaic conditional 'if', followed by the nominal phrase همه ز آهنی ('if all are of iron'), where ز = از indicates material/quality and آهنی = 'made of iron'. Verb forms are classical (optative/conditional sense).
segments.s440-g134: اگر is the conditional 'if' (here introducing an alternative/hypothesis). دین‌پرستی is a deverbal noun 'religious devotion / being a believer', and آهرمنی is an adjective/noun 'Ahrimanic, demonic'. The particle ور functions classically to link alternatives (≈ 'or'); this is an archaic coordination meaning 'whether ... or ...'.
segments.s440-g135: تو is the explicit subject 'you'. تا زنده‌ای is a temporal/subordinate clause ('as long as you are alive'), with زنده‌ای = present 2nd‑person predicate. سوی نیکی is a prepositional phrase 'toward goodness', and گرای is an imperative/2nd‑person present form 'incline/turn' (classical usage meaning 'turn yourself toward...').
segments.s440-g136: مگر here introduces a concessive/conditional sense ('perhaps' / 'unless' depending on nuance). کام یابی is a 2nd‑person present/subjunctive/optative ('you may find/attain your desire'); به دیگر سرای is a prepositional phrase 'in another house/abode' (سرای = abode, i.e. another realm). The verb yāb‑ form expresses potential/hope rather than a plain factual present.
segments.s440-g9: همی is the classical iterative/imperfective marker (‘kept, was repeatedly’). ریخت is a past/preterite form here used with همی to express continuous action ‘kept letting fall / scattering’. The prepositional cluster از بر = ‘from off/of’ governs یال (‘mane’) and خاک (‘dust’) is the thing being let fall—literally ‘kept letting dust fall from off (his) mane’. Word order (verb + subject) is idiomatic poetic word order.
segments.s440-g10: همی‌کرد is the iterative imperfective of کردن (‘kept doing’). Here کرد is a light verb combining with the noun چاک to form the action ‘to make a gash / rend’. روی is the direct target (‘his face’), and بر خویش چاک is a locative/affected phrase ‘a gash on himself’ where خوی + pronominal suffix ش = ‘his own’. The conjunction و links two coordinated affected parts (face and himself/clothes/body).
segments.s440-g11: همی‌گفت is iterative ‘kept saying / he kept crying’. زار‌ای is an interjection/lament (‘woefully/alas’) with the vocative particle ای; گو here functions as a verbal gerund/quotative ‘saying’ (introducing what is said) and is followed by the name/address پیلتن. In classical Persian direct speech is often introduced without punctuation; گو marks the speaker’s words.
segments.s440-g12: نخواهم is a negative volitive/present (‘I do not want / I will not’). که introduces the subordinate clause and governs the present-subjunctive/potential پوشد (here ‘be covered / be clothed’). تنم = تن + ـم is ‘my body’ (possessive suffix). جز is the exclusive preposition ‘except’, and کفن is the noun ‘shroud’. The structure expresses refusal: ‘I will not have my body clothed except in a shroud.’
segments.s440-g13: گو here is an imperative/quotative ‘say / call (him)’. What follows is a titulary noun phrase: سرفراز (an epithet ‘proud / honorable’) + اژدهای (head noun ‘dragon’) + دلیر (postpositive adjective ‘brave’). Poetic word order allows the epithets to surround the head noun.
segments.s440-g14: زواره is a proper name here; که introduces a relative clause ‘who’. بد modifies the following predicate and functions adverbially (‘badly / ill-’), and نامبردار in this older sense means ‘one who is named / called’ (so بد نامبردار = ‘ill-called’). شیر is the name/word he is ill-called by — ‘lion’. So the clause says ‘Zowareh, who is ill-called “lion.”’
segments.s440-g15: شغاد is a proper name (the villain Shaghad) — the provided lemma senses do not include the proper-name reading, so treat it as a name. آن is the demonstrative ‘that’. بنفرین here is an exclamatory adjective/curse meaning ‘accursed’ (or the speaker’s imprecation), and شوریده‌بخت is a compound adjective ‘ill-fated / wretched’. The whole phrase is a curse apposition: ‘that accursed, ill-fated Shaghad’.
segments.s440-g16: بکند is the 3rd-person form of کردن used optatively/jussively here (‘may he do / he will cause / he shall’), expressing the speaker’s wish or imprecation. از بن = ‘from the root/of the root’ (از marks separation), این خسروانی درخت = ‘this royal/kingly tree’ (خسروانی adjectival). The verb takes the whole following noun phrase as its object: ‘(he) will tear up this royal tree from the root.’
segments.s440-g65: نمانم is a first-person negative perfect/past form meaning “I did not leave / I did not let remain.” The phrase ز ایشان is a source/genitive phrase “of/from them,” یکی را is the definite object “one” marked by را, and به جای is a prepositional phrase “in place of / instead of.” Word order reflects epic/poetic inversion: verb first, then source and object, then the ‘in place of’ phrase.
segments.s440-g66: هم‌انکس is a demonstrative/ emphatic noun phrase “that very person.” که introduces a relative clause “who/that,” بود is the past copula “was,” اندرین = از + این (poetic) means “in this / here / among these,” and رهنمای is the noun “guide.” Together the clause modifies هم‌انکس: “the very person who was the guide here.”
segments.s440-g67: بفرمود is the honorific/past verb “he ordered/commanded/said” used here to introduce an instruction. تا introduces a purpose/finite clause (‘that/so that’). تختهای گران is the object phrase “heavy/precious thrones” (تختهای = plural ‘thrones,’ گران = ‘heavy/valuable’), the thing ordered to be brought.
segments.s440-g68: بیارند is an old/formal subjunctive/present imperatival form meaning “(that) they bring.” از هر سوی is an adverbial phrase “from every side / from every direction.” در here is the noun “door / gate” (not the preposition), modified by گران ‘heavy/valuable’; together در گران = “heavy gates.” The clause continues the commanded list of things to be brought.
segments.s440-g69: ببردند is a past-tense verb “they carried / they took.” بسیار here functions as a quantifier/adverb “many / much.” با هوی is an instrumental phrase “with clamor/excitement/zeal” (هوی = passion/excitement). The conjunction و links to تخت, which is resumed by the next verb; syntactically the clause says they took many things with shouting and (they) the throne (is continued).
segments.s440-g70: نهادند is the past verb “they placed / they set.” بر تخت زیبا is a locative phrase “on the beautiful throne” (بر = on/upon, تخت = throne, زیبا = beautiful). درخت here is the noun “wood / timber,” and the phrase is ambiguous: it can mean they placed wood upon the beautiful throne, or that the throne itself is made/laid with wood (a wooden throne).
segments.s440-g71: گشاد modifies میان: گشاد آن میان = “that wide opening/space/fold.” بستن is the verbal noun/infinitive “(the) closing / to close” or the notion of a fastening; here it appears with پهلوی “side” to describe the action on the side seam/side of the garment (i.e. opening/undoing the side). The phrase describes making/opening that wide side-fold.
segments.s440-g72: برآهیخت is a past tense verb meaning “he raised / lifted up / rose up” and here functions transitively. ز + و is the poetic contraction ز(o) = از او “from him” (preposition + pronoun). جامه خسروی is the noun phrase “the kingly/royal garment.” The whole segment means he lifted/removed the royal robe from him; note the contracted poetic form زو for ‘from him.’
segments.s440-g17: که داند که با پیل روباه شوم is a doubled-complement structure: 'که داند' (rhetorical 'who knows') introduces the subordinate clause beginning with the second 'که' (complementizer). با marks accompaniment/instrument ('with'), followed by the two nouns پیل and روباه placed side by side (poetic juxtaposition meaning 'elephant and fox' or powerful and cunning forces). شوم is the present-subjunctive/optative of شدن ('to become') expressing possibility ('I may become' or 'I might be made'); the subject is not written and is first person by verb form.
segments.s440-g18: همی is an emphatic/continuous adverb ('indeed, even now/still'); کین is the nominative subject meaning 'hatred/vengeance'; سگالد is a verbal root meaning 'to set dogs on / to harry' used here as the verb (3rd-person action). بران is a locative/adverbial ('upon that' / 'on it'), and مرز و بوم is a compound object 'border and homeland/realm'. The clause thus says that hatred presently drives or sets dogs upon that border and land.
segments.s440-g19: که دارد به یاد این‌چنین روزگار begins again with the complementizer 'که' ('who/that'). دارد is the present tense 'has/keeps'; به یاد is the fixed prepositional phrase 'in memory / in mind of'; این‌چنین modifies روزگار ('such / of this sort'), so the clause means 'who keeps such times in mind' or 'who remembers such days.'
segments.s440-g20: که داند شنیدن ز آموزگار uses داند ('knows') plus the infinitival/masdar شنیدن ('to hear' / 'to be told') to indicate knowing by hearing or being able to learn by hearing. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and آموزگار is the source 'teacher'. The whole means roughly 'who can say one knows it by hearing from a teacher' or 'who could learn this from a teacher's telling.'
segments.s440-g21: که چون رستمی پیش بینم به خاک: 'که' again opens the clause; چون here functions as a temporal 'when/as' introducing the circumstance. رستمی is the proper name Rostam in apposition; پیش + بینم ('before me I see') places Rostam in front of the speaker. به خاک is a directional/result phrase 'to the ground' (i.e. fall to the earth). بینم is 1st-person present subjunctive/indicative 'I see' used here in the subordinate temporal clause.
segments.s440-g22: به گفتار روباه گردد هلاک: به before گفتار marks instrument/agental force ('by/through the speech'). گفتار 'words/speech' + روباه 'of the fox' names the cause. گردد is the classical third-person present/subjunctive of گشتن 'become' (here passive/resultative), and هلاک is the resultant predicate 'ruin/destruction' — 'he becomes destroyed.'
segments.s440-g23: چرا پیش ایشان نمردم به زار is a lamenting rhetorical question. چرا 'why'; پیش ایشان 'in their presence / before them'; نمردم is the negated past 'I did not die' (نمردم = ن+مردم); به زار is an adverbial phrase 'in wretchedness / in lamentation' (زَار used as manner). The speaker asks why he did not die lamenting before those people.
segments.s440-g24: چرا ماندم اندر جهان یادگار continues the question: چرا 'why'; ماندم is the past 'I remained/was left'; اندر جهان 'in the world / in this life'; یادگار 'a memento / memorial' — here predicative: 'why was I left in the world as a remembrance?'

sentences.s440-s21: He brought the slain body back out of the well and brought the world to lamentation and supplication.
sentences.s440-s22: When Faramarz reached near Kabul, he saw no fame in the inner city.
sentences.s440-s23: The whole city was fleeing, had become weeping, consumed by the mourning for Jahangir.
sentences.s440-s24: He came to that hunting plain, to a place where they had dug a well.
sentences.s440-s25: When the brave son beheld his father's face, he let out a roar like a lion.
sentences.s440-s26: In that manner a body, full of blood, lay upon the earth, thrown down and overthrown.
sentences.s440-s27: He said (aloud), 'O lofty champion, in your sight who has brought such harm.'
sentences.s440-s28: May a curse be upon that fearless man; may dust be upon his head in place of (his) skull.
sentences.s440-s57: They built a sepulchre in the inner garden, and lifted its head up into the clouds.
sentences.s440-s58: They set two golden thrones opposite one another, and placed on them the sleeper—O fortunate one.
sentences.s440-s59: Whoever was among the worshippers, among the free and the pure‑hearted servants,
sentences.s440-s60: They mixed musk with flowers, and poured it at the foot of the place called Piltan.
sentences.s440-s37: First they washed him with warm blood, gently over his brow, mane, beard, and body.
sentences.s440-s38: They burned ambergris and saffron, and they sewed up all his weariness.
sentences.s440-s39: They poured rosewater upon his (tarkash) and spread pure camphor over his body.
sentences.s440-s40: They adorned his body in brocade; thereafter they used rose, musk, and wine.
sentences.s440-s17: Where is that heart and counsel and bright spirit? Where is that might, loftiness, and weighty mane?
sentences.s440-s18: Where is that great, dragon-emblazoned standard? Where are the arrow, Gopala, and the purple sword?
sentences.s440-s19: You did not remain in the world and you went into the earth; may your enemy’s head be in the abyss.
sentences.s440-s20: Then thereupon he sent Faramarz with an army, so that he might seek battle from the king.
sentences.s440-s1: From those famed men a horseman sprang forth; sometimes he dismounted, sometimes he mounted again.
sentences.s440-s2: When he came toward Zabulistan he said that the elephant had been laid with the earth (i.e. had fallen/dead).
sentences.s440-s3: Zowara himself and his army likewise remained; no horseman sprang forth because of ill suspicion.
sentences.s440-s4: A clamor arose from Zabulistan — from an ill-wisher and from the king of Kabulistan.
sentences.s440-s49: They departed — the wakeful‑hearted — to other places; they cut away from him the heavy thrones.
sentences.s440-s50: For two days the time was taken up with that affair; they loaded Rakhsh's body onto an elephant.
sentences.s440-s51: From Kabulistan to Zabulistan the land became like a place of tumult.
sentences.s440-s52: Women and men stood there sorrowfully at (his) feet; there was not even room on the ground for a single body.
sentences.s440-s45: He lifted his brother from his place and then hurried to the spot where he had seen him weary.
sentences.s440-s46: Musk and camphor above it and rosewater beneath — thus he poured them on the sleeping-place.
sentences.s440-s47: After that he lifted Rakhsh's body, washed it, and then spread the garments.
sentences.s440-s48: They washed it and fashioned a brocade shroud; they sought a place — a certain elm.
sentences.s440-s13: Why should I live, and for what time? Why should I sleep and have a tomb?
sentences.s440-s14: Then he began a great lament; when he (or they) played a tune beside Pour‑Pahlu he made a song.
sentences.s440-s15: Gavā Shirgīrā Yela Mahtara — brave, world‑wise, and pestilence‑bringing.
sentences.s440-s16: Where is that bravery and manhood? Where is that greatness and wisdom?
sentences.s440-s29: By God and by your life, O renowned one; by the dust of Nariman and Sam the horseman.
sentences.s440-s30: that no one shall ever see my body except (clad) in armor, with its sash untied and its knot laden with snow.
sentences.s440-s31: Know this, then, that I will take vengeance — I shall go to Piltan — on that faithless assembly.
sentences.s440-s32: That very man who, with him, bound this enmity between them and came to us to speak of it.
sentences.s440-s53: They took up two coffins in their hands; amid the throng they imagined him gone like the wind.
sentences.s440-s54: After ten days and ten nights he reached Zabul; no one saw him laid upon the ground.
sentences.s440-s55: The world was shaken by his pain with an outcry; it was said that the Hamun (the plain) rose in a boiling surge.
sentences.s440-s56: No one heard anyone's voice; all the lands lamented, and that was enough.
sentences.s440-s41: The shroud-maker let blood rain down upon him; that camphor-colored beard fell upon his shoulder.
sentences.s440-s42: There was no place for his body even upon two couches; could it be called a body, if (it were) a shade-spreading tree?
sentences.s440-s43: They made a fine coffin of saj wood, with golden nails and an ivory lining.
sentences.s440-s44: All its seams were sealed with pitch; it was anointed with pitch, musk, and ambergris.
sentences.s440-s61: Everyone kept saying, "O renowned one, why did you wish to offer musk and amber?"
sentences.s440-s62: You will have no kingship nor feasting; you will not don (festal garb), and likewise you will lie (i.e., fall) on the field of battle.
sentences.s440-s63: You will give neither treasure nor dinars; verily these things became worthless before you.
sentences.s440-s64: Now be joyful in the blissful Paradise, for your God formed you from justice and manliness.
sentences.s440-s65: They shut (him) in the tomb and departed; that famed, proud-necked lion then appeared.
sentences.s440-s66: What are you seeking from this sponge‑house, which from the beginning is suffering and whose end is suffering?
sentences.s440-s67: You would cast (them) to the earth if they were all of iron — whether you are a worshipper of religion or of Ahriman.
sentences.s440-s68: As long as you are alive, incline toward goodness; perhaps you will attain your desire in another abode.
sentences.s440-s5: Zal kept letting dust fall from his mane; he kept tearing at his face and gashing himself.
sentences.s440-s6: He kept crying out, 'Alas — O Piltan!' / 'I will not have my body covered with anything but a shroud.'
sentences.s440-s7: Call him 'Sarfraz, the brave dragon' — Zowareh, who is ill-called 'lion.'
sentences.s440-s8: May that Shaghad — accursed, ill-fated — uproot this royal tree from its root.
sentences.s440-s33: I did not leave any one of them in place of that very person who had been their guide here.
sentences.s440-s34: He commanded that heavy thrones be brought and that heavy gates be brought from every direction.

sentences.s440-s36: He parted/opened that wide side (of the fold) and lifted the kingly garment from him.
sentences.s440-s9: Who can tell that, with elephant and fox, I may become so; indeed, hatred will set hounds upon that border and land.
sentences.s440-s10: Who keeps in mind such days; who could learn (of them) by hearing from a teacher.
sentences.s440-s11: Who knows — when I behold Rostam before me, he may fall to the ground and, by the fox's words, become destroyed.
sentences.s440-s12: Why did I not die before them in lamentation? Why did I remain in the world as a memorial?
